title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure projects and discussed utility operations. Key actions included awarding a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, approving a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to develop enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines of $100 per day.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs. A Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting was scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported on road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, spring curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Dewatering operations were conducted near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to flooding. Jones Street culvert failures required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow systems and servicing mowers. Kennedy Park Farmers Market parking received positive feedback with no reported issues.

Josh reported on water service repair at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and private well sampling. Significant clear water infiltration was identified, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers as a major contributor. Water main flushing notices were being mailed with work starting the following week.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield/Rib Mountain Metro area. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and grant funding helps one-time costs but not recurring expenses like street maintenance and snow plowing. Wisconsin is relatively affordable for automobile ownership compared to neighboring states. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton with estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Michael noted this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to purchasing material, estimating $130,000 in savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing violations for illegal sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Current ordinance allows fines of $100 to $1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Staff was directed to create enforcement language starting at $100 per day for first offenses. An INI study by In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Extension Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented agreement for a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect to water and sewer mains via long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added requirement that reconstruction occur no later than 2029, at which time the owner will pay assessment for main extension. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for construction on Military Road from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share covers water main relocation ($88,704) and sanitary sewer ($6,156) totaling $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek intersection project, MSA was selected at $37,315 over Clark Dietz ($48,910). For Bladel Avenue project, Clark Dietz was selected at $73,730 over MSA ($70,400) due to having full-time staff rather than summer interns. Motion passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Authorize water/sewer extension agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline no later than 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 Weston share)
Award Alderson/Jelinek construction services to MSA and Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to investigate street lighting for Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance for the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Grant request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously by voice vote.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously by voice vote.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members asked about hotel room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year when they received $5,000. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with Jackson seconding the motion. Passed unanimously. Commission requested better hotel room tracking data in their post-event report.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. She mentioned this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s, and candidates were impressed with the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest requested to provide accurate hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. The commission also scheduled an initial steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update for June 1st.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline easement issue, which reduced fill costs significantly. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth, up from current staffing of 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. An alternate storage shed was discussed as a bid alternate depending on budget. Commission members discussed ensuring the shed placement allows for future building expansion to the north. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and drainage, and one winter operational inspection focused on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. The commission approved the program with a requirement to report back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee. Motion passed unanimously.
Motion to approve as presented was made and seconded. Passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email confirmations and calendar invites to members.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, noting some zoning complaints have gone unresolved for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with requirement to report back to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility on Sternberg about wellhead protection conditional use compliance
Steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan update scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts with commission

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on besides approval of minutes; the meeting focused on introducing department heads and explaining committee structure and responsibilities.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (Chair Boots and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - none for this meeting
Educational presentations - Review of standing committee ordinances and responsibilities
Health Department overview presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department overview by Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center overview by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date discussion and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinances and responsibilities
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction over the Health Department, Social Services Department, Veteran Service Department, North Central Health Care, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He detailed the various subordinate boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee would be working on the new strategic plan with a goal of completion by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect the public from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health (1,200+ licenses), nursing/communicable disease, family health, and community health improvement. She highlighted new initiatives including public health vending machines with free tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new homeless system response position funded through partnership with the City of Wausau. Administrator Leonard noted approximately half of the department's $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with the remainder from intergovernmental grants and fees for service.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who could not attend. The department has nearly 140 FTE and a 2026 budget of over $22 million, with about $7 million from tax levy. Key divisions include child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, noting many clients had never had a bank account or been on a formal lease before. He indicated potential restructuring requests may come during budget season due to increased needs in children's long-term support and child support units.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his department increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE as of 2025. Customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. He highlighted the PACT Act's impact in increasing claims and the $44 million annual compensation flowing to Marathon County veterans. New initiatives include partnership with Center for Veterans Issues for veteran housing (serving 30-70 veterans through HUD-VASH vouchers), suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and education for hospice facilities on proper death certificate documentation for surviving spouse benefits. In 2025, veterans received $2.6 million in retroactive payments. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and that regionalizing has been opposed.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county organization (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) serving as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. The ADRC has approximately 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. Key services include information/assistance, options counseling, caregiver support, dementia care specialists, falls prevention, benefit specialists, and Meals on Wheels. Ray noted significant financial challenges as nutrition program costs have risen 40-45% over four years while federal Older American Act funding has remained flat since 2024. The organization requested its first levy increase since 2011 formation. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meals program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas
Chair Boots to present strategic plan information at upcoming meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
New business including committee appointments and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 per duplex to $563, and from $250 per multifamily to $495, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the justification for the numbers, stating there is no real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Clark supported the fee increase, noting population growth requires more park equipment and space. Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 both passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Discussion centered on removing specific reasons for remote attendance (health, safety, welfare) to make the policy broader. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy by striking sentences requiring justification for remote attendance. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Herling and Lobsinger. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside. Under this system, a fire inspection by any participating department results in a sticker allowing the food truck to operate in all participating communities without additional inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Golden Mua may be considered for Finance committee after gaining experience on CLIPS. Motion by Herling, second by Lobsinger, passed unanimously. The board agreed that future committee applicant resumes should be included in board packets.

The board discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about vehicles parking on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors prefer having vehicles with them. Mont Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to poor visibility and distance from traffic. The board acknowledged this may require ongoing adjustments and could potentially be discontinued if no suitable location is found.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and secondhand jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained these licenses protect against stolen merchandise sales. An amendment was added requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Passed unanimously as amended.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan reported on the ongoing speed limit issue. An engineering study showed the road should be 35 mph, but traffic data showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. A trustee noted the speed limit has been inconsistent with the ordinance for over two months. Dolan indicated this would need to come back to the board, possibly at the joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during general public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He criticized the administrator for not filling the role and questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before being officially appointed. He also complained about lack of Kennedy Park information in meeting packets. Pitel also spoke during the park impact fee public hearing questioning the justification for the fee amounts without detailed park maintenance cost data.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with passage of Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM as joint meeting with Plan Commission
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle Township building inspections assistance
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to the board
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected two at-large members to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard emotional public testimony from affected Ringle residents requesting fee waivers, and administration committed to exploring additional relief options through the Community Foundation.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments by Chair with remarks on leadership responsibilities
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees for disaster relief
Motion to suspend rules for disaster relief resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks emphasizing that 34% of the board is in its first or second term and appointments were guided by goals including increasing diverse perspectives and ensuring leadership development opportunities. Vice chairs were assigned responsibility to shepherd the strategic plan. The motion to approve committee appointments by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Mask noted the system marked him absent without allowing him to vote, clarifying he would have voted aye.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, stating she has watched her advocate for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for tornado and flood debris. The standard $65/ton gate rate would be reduced to $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris, with the state waiving $13.10 and the Town of Ringle waiving $2.33. Leonard noted Marathon County will not meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle to qualify. He mentioned preliminary conversations with the Community Foundation about reimbursing landowners for unreimbursed insurance costs. The Solid Waste Department processed approximately 400 tons of debris, including opening on Sunday. Director Hagen Busher cautioned that Dane County lost FEMA reimbursement in the 1990s when they waived fees entirely. After public comment and extensive board discussion about extending timelines and tracking costs, the board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to fully waive its fees for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees and that private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts. She stated Marathon County has not provided significant financial assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting 44 homes were affected, some insurance adjusters have not yet arrived, and residents need more time and relief from fees.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation about reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Board to revisit tipping fee reduction at May meeting if extension beyond state declaration period is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive septic system reconnect fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Rezoning petition 5A1: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract - Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley
Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck providing the second. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A1: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the rezoning request from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. The motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff recommendations for waiving county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future disasters. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness for individual disaster victims not in declared disaster areas. Marilyn Bend asked about mound system replacement costs. After significant debate, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment, which passed. The amended resolution then passed on a non-unanimous vote to forward to the county board.

Director Dave Hagenbusher provided an overview of the solid waste department, highlighting the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032 due to PFAS regulations, and significant capital expenses for landfill expansion and closure. He noted hauling costs for April were close to $90,000 due to significant rainfall and explained the department operates as an enterprise fund covering costs through tipping fees.

County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both county and City of Wausau park systems. He highlighted that Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld acknowledged this would be Lovelin's last committee meeting before his June 1st retirement after 35 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as the new county forest administrator.

actionItems: Rezoning approved for James Offer and David Tract property from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning approved for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan - forwarded to County Board
Amended resolution to waive only septic system reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 tornado forwarded to County Board
Conservation services presentation to be scheduled for summer meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month due to the chair's absence, and approved signage for the Yellow Banks kayak launch system. The meeting featured significant public comment criticizing the Kennedy Park fundraising transparency and the 4 p.m. meeting time.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Topics for future meetings and adjournment

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded, with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously.

Director Sean Oster provided an update on Kennedy Park, presenting a timeline document from project inception through the May 9th ribbon cutting. He explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for field lights, having approximately $84-85,000 of the $117,000 goal at the community foundation. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital dollars and fundraising timelines. Oster confirmed no village capital funds are currently earmarked for Kennedy Park as those items were pulled pending the project. The Friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mats were provided by TC Energy (formerly ANR Pipeline/ATC) and the village retained some extra mats for potential trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy from 6504 [street name unclear] spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff discussed challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches along the trail, noting limited equipment access prevents bringing a pressure washer. Director Oster mentioned trying various graffiti removers with limited success. Committee members suggested anti-vandalism coatings or seal coats, and discussed potential volunteer workdays. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Ringo from Marathon County Parks Foundation (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed kayak launch signs for three Weston locations: Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks. The signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and donation URLs. Ringo noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation carried unanimously.

Director Oster mentioned receiving an email requesting food allergy awareness signs for playgrounds. Committee members agreed to bring this back for future discussion and education, acknowledging mixed feelings about setting precedent for similar requests.

publicComment: Two public comments were received. A resident named Andy from 6504 [street unclear] spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 Street, Weston), calling in under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park funding transparency, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event funds were being tracked. He suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation at three Weston locations
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and potential anti-vandalism coatings for trail amenities
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11, 5-7 p.m. in training room
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved meeting minutes and acknowledged monthly financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation by Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and the quarterly newsletter. Committee member Evan Erling announced he would be stepping down from the committee.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Erling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried with all in favor, no opposition.

discussions.item: TIF one and TIF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the final numbers for TIF closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, noting the bonds are due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the numbers look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of the clerk's office operations with three staff members (two full-time, one part-time). She covered elections administration including Badger Book electronic poll books ($11,000 for four units), explaining 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is processed through the clerk's office walk-ins. Discussion included chief inspector pay potentially increasing from current rate to $15/hour, pet licensing fees doubling from $10 to $20 at Marathon County's request, and the quarterly newsletter costing approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage to 5,089 postal patrons.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with her arriving at 6 AM the last three Saturdays to help vendors. The market has expanded to include food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft people. Vendors expressed happiness with the new setup and location. Discussion addressed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff noting parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson while the east side remains no parking.

Evan Erling announced he would be stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA resolution to call TIF bonds expected this summer for October 1st bond payoff
Potential chief inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be considered at next election term
Seek citizen volunteers for open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th and took several actions including recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for a dilapidated property. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations.

agenda.item: Call to order, pledge of allegiance, and roll call
Written communications and department reports (code enforcement, fire/EMS, police)
Request for crosswalk on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke about concerns with the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, stating it was 'terrible execution' and predicting grass damage from vehicle parking. He raised concerns about ADA-compliant parking being placed on grass instead of asphalt, questioned the need for new food truck fire inspections, and criticized the lack of follow-up on longstanding code violations.

Staff reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from code enforcement after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

Committee reviewed final copy of nuisance amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways) with language added requiring compliance with jurisdiction authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to board of trustees passed unanimously.

Committee discussed model e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) from roadways, sidewalks, and trails due to safety concerns, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion addressed electric scooters used by middle schoolers, with committee agreeing to leave officer discretion for minor play vehicles. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking restriction (4am-4pm, October 31-May 1, even/odd) is far outside what neighboring municipalities have (typically 5-hour windows, 2am-7am). Director Mike Wodalski suggested eliminating village-wide restrictions and signing only 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to allow officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee deferred action, directing staff to identify specific problem areas for July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested crosswalk between church and DC Everest school parking lot. Committee discussed costs ($2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system) and noted no accidents have occurred. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously.

Fire department presented regional fire inspection program with Stevens Point, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. One inspection with sticker valid across all participating municipalities. Fire Inspector Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue private drive. Chief Hunt added Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski has addressing issues with eight apartments having separate addresses plus utility room. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to proceed with raze order for abandoned structure. Building has been abandoned since 2010-2014, was too deteriorated for fire training exercise, and was found housing chickens in 2021. Current owners prefer village proceed with demolition with costs added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke for approximately 4 minutes expressing concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, including potential grass damage from parking, ADA parking placement on grass instead of asphalt, questioning the need for food truck fire inspections, mobile food vendor regulations on public property, and lack of visible progress on longstanding code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to board of trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to board of trustees
Staff to prepare overnight parking regulation options with specific problem areas for July meeting
Staff to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if agreed, recommend approval to board
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to board of trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
